title: Hidradenitis Suppurativa
oneLiner: Chronic inflammatory skin disease of apocrine gland-bearing areas causing painful nodules, abscesses and sinus tracts; associated with obesity, smoking, and metabolic syndrome.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Severe secondary cellulitis / sepsis** – if fever, rigors, or rapidly spreading erythema with systemic toxicity (NEWS2 ≥5), treat as sepsis before assuming routine HS
- **Necrotising fasciitis** – single finding: crepitus, bullae, or severe pain out of proportion to exam (rare but lethal mimic; get urgent surgical review)
- **Acute arterial occlusion** – in buttock/perineal HS, sudden unilateral severe pain + pulselessness suggests embolic event (check pulses, doppler)
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Recurrent, painful “boils” or abscesses in axillae, groin, perineum, or inframammary folds
  - Chronic malodorous discharge from sinus tracts
  - Itching, burning, and restricted movement (especially if in axillae)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Multiple, deep-seated nodules and abscesses at different stages (inflamed, draining, scarred) in bilateral apocrine areas
  - Comedones (open and closed) – pathognomonic double-ended comedones (“tombstone” comedones)
  - Sinus tracts that are “ropelike” and bridged scarring
  - Positive “Tunnelling” sign: gentle compression produces purulent discharge from distal opening
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Furunculosis / simple boils** – no comedones, no sinus tracts, no bilateral symmetry; single lesion resolves with incision/drainage
- **Crohn’s disease with perianal fistulae** – look for GI symptoms (diarrhoea, weight loss); HS never involves perianal mucosa unless Crohn’s coexists
- **Perianal abscess / fistula-in-ano** – distinct anal gland origin; digital rectal exam reveals internal opening; HS usually spares the anal canal
- **Epidermoid cyst** – solitary, slow-growing, non-inflammatory unless ruptured; no recurrent crops
- **Lymphogranuloma venereum or Behçet’s disease** – sexual history and genital ulcers help differentiate
- **Splitter:** the presence of **double-ended comedones** in typical apocrine sites (axillae, groin) is virtually diagnostic of HS
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** 
  - Swab for culture (pus or draining sinus) to guide antibiotics if secondary infection suspected
  - Pain score (0–10) – HS is notoriously painful
- **Bloods:** 
  - FBC, CRP, ESR – often elevated during flares, but nonspecific
  - HbA1c, fasting glucose – HS is strongly associated with diabetes / insulin resistance
  - Lipid profile, BP – screen for metabolic syndrome
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** none routinely; ultrasound (high-frequency) can define sinus tract depth and exclude abscess collection if planning drainage
  - **Second-line:** MRI with contrast for complex disease (peri‑anal, perineal) to map sinus tracts before surgery
- **Special tests:** 
  - Consider biopsy if atypical (e.g., unilateral, no comedones, or persistent ulcer to exclude squamous cell carcinoma in chronic lesions)
- **Diagnostic criteria/scoring system:**
  - **Hurley staging (simplest for ward):**
    - **Stage I:** single or multiple abscesses without sinus tracts or scarring
    - **Stage II:** recurrent abscesses with sinus tracts and scarring, widely separated
    - **Stage III:** diffuse involvement with multiple interconnected sinus tracts and abscesses across entire region
  - **Sartorius severity score** (research) – counts nodules, sinuses, longest distance between lesions
- **Never-miss test:** **HbA1c** – undiagnosed diabetes is common in HS; miss it and you miss a modifiable risk factor and comorbidity
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If signs of sepsis (NEWS2 ≥5, fever, tachycardia): take blood cultures, start broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (e.g., co‑amoxiclav 1.2 g TDS or piperacillin‑tazobactam per local policy) after discussion with microbiology
  - Pain relief: paracetamol 1 g QDS + ibuprofen 400 mg TDS (if no contraindication) – many patients undermedicate
  - Incise and drain only if a fluctuant abscess with clear point of rupture and no deeper sinus tract; otherwise avoid – can worsen tracts
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **Mild (Hurley I):** topical clindamycin 1% lotion twice daily for 12 weeks; avoid long-term systemic antibiotics
  - **Moderate–severe (Hurley II–III):** 
    - Oral antibiotic combination: **clindamycin 300 mg twice daily + rifampicin 300 mg twice daily for 10 weeks** (NICE recommended)
    - Continue for minimum 10 weeks; reassess response at 4 weeks
    - If poor response: consider **adalimumab** (biologic; see Drugs section)
  - Wound care: gentle cleansing with saline, non-adherent dressings (e.g., Mepitel), avoid packing of sinus tracts – can extend tracts
  - Smoking cessation advice – proven to reduce flares
  - Weight management referral – obesity is a major driver
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Dermatology referral for all Hurley II–III
  - Biologics (adalimumab – first-line biologic in UK) or surgery (deroofing, local excision, or wide excision for advanced disease)
  - Consider oral tetracyclines (doxycycline 100 mg once daily for 3 months) as second-line if clindamycin/rifampicin not tolerated
  - Metformin (off-label) for insulin resistance (discuss with endocrinology)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** 
  - Fluids: if septic – 0.9% saline 500 mL stat, reassess; else maintenance fluids only if NBM
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) if BMI >30 (common in HS) – dose adjustment may be needed
  - Diet: normal, unless awaiting surgery
  - Observations: baseline and then per NEWS2, every 4 hours if stable
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line (moderate–severe):** 
  - **Clindamycin 300 mg orally twice daily + Rifampicin 300 mg orally twice daily for 10 weeks** (NICE CG157)
  - **Adalimumab** (when Hurley II–III and failed antibiotics): 
    - Loading dose: 160 mg SC at week 0, then 80 mg SC at week 2, then **40 mg weekly** from week 4
    - Pre-screen: TB, hepatitis B, HIV; monitor for infections
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Doxycycline 100 mg once daily** for 12 weeks (second-line, less effective)
  - **Erythromycin 500 mg twice daily** (if pregnancy or penicillin allergy)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Rifampicin: potent enzyme inducer; reduces efficacy of oral contraceptives, warfarin, many anticonvulsants – always check interactions
  - Clindamycin: risk of *Clostridium difficile* colitis – stop if diarrhoea develops
  - Adalimumab: avoid live vaccines; caution in active infections
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** 
  - Clindamycin: no dose adjustment needed in renal impairment; use caution in severe hepatic impairment (reduce dose by 50%)
  - Rifampicin: no adjustment in renal or hepatic
  - Adalimumab: no renal adjustment; caution in hepatic impairment (risk of hepatitis)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Rifampicin given as monotherapy** or short course (<10 weeks) – rapidly induces resistance; ALWAYS combine with clindamycin and complete full course
  - **Adalimumab loading dose given as 40 mg weekly from start** – leads to underdosing; ensure correct loading: 160 mg at week 0, 80 mg week 2, then 40 mg weekly
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Secondary infection: cellulitis, abscess, sepsis
  - Flare-related: severe pain, immobility, depression (high suicide risk)
  - Medication side effects: *C. difficile* colitis (clindamycin), hepatotoxicity (rifampicin), injection site reactions (adalimumab)
- **Chronic:**
  - Scarring: keloid, contractures (axillary or perineal, limiting movement)
  - Squamous cell carcinoma (rare, but high mortality) developing in chronic sinus tracts – any non-healing ulcer, especially in perineal disease, must be biopsied
  - Anaemia of chronic disease
  - Metabolic syndrome, cardiovascular events (increased risk)
  - Psychosocial: depression, social isolation, unemployment
sections.Prognosis:
- Chronic relapsing disease with no cure; quality of life scores often as poor as in heart failure or severe psoriasis
- Early intervention (biologics, surgery) improves control; 30–50% of patients achieve remission with adalimumab
- Increased all-cause mortality (mainly cardiovascular and suicide)
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check HbA1c and fasting lipids** when you make the diagnosis – HS is a cutaneous marker of metabolic syndrome
- **Offer smoking cessation** at every interaction – a single most effective non-drug intervention
- **Refer to dermatology early** (Hurley II or III) – delays in biologics worsen scarring and outcome
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not incise and drain every lesion** – it can create new sinus tracts and worsen disease; only drain fluctuant, tense abscesses if point of rupture
- **Do not treat with short-course antibiotics** (e.g., 7 days of flucloxacillin) – HS requires 10 weeks of combination therapy (clindamycin + rifampicin) for moderate–severe
- **Do not dismiss as “just boils”** – HS has profound physical and psychological impact; take patient’s pain seriously and screen for depression
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** 
  - Spreading redness, fever, or rigors (signs of cellulitis/sepsis)
  - Unable to pass urine (associated with urethral stricture from perineal HS)
  - New severe pain or foul-smelling discharge (possible secondary infection)
- **Follow-up:**
  - Dermatology outpatient within 4 weeks for initial management plan (sooner if starting adalimumab)
  - General practice for regular monitoring of comorbidities (diabetes, lipids, BP)
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / medical team if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (especially if fever >38.5°C and CRP >100)
  - Signs of sepsis (lactate >2, hypotension) – start sepsis 6 within 1 hour
  - Suspicion of necrotising fasciitis (pain out of proportion, crepitus, bullae) – immediate surgical review
  - Suspected *C. difficile* colitis (diarrhoea after clindamycin) – stool culture and stop antibiotic
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or any single Red Score (respiratory rate >25, SPO2 <91%, systolic BP <90, new confusion)
  - Septic shock requiring vasopressors

rawMarkdown:
## Hidradenitis Suppurativa

**One-liner:** Chronic inflammatory skin disease of apocrine gland-bearing areas causing painful nodules, abscesses and sinus tracts; associated with obesity, smoking, and metabolic syndrome.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Severe secondary cellulitis / sepsis** – if fever, rigors, or rapidly spreading erythema with systemic toxicity (NEWS2 ≥5), treat as sepsis before assuming routine HS
- **Necrotising fasciitis** – single finding: crepitus, bullae, or severe pain out of proportion to exam (rare but lethal mimic; get urgent surgical review)
- **Acute arterial occlusion** – in buttock/perineal HS, sudden unilateral severe pain + pulselessness suggests embolic event (check pulses, doppler)

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Recurrent, painful “boils” or abscesses in axillae, groin, perineum, or inframammary folds
  - Chronic malodorous discharge from sinus tracts
  - Itching, burning, and restricted movement (especially if in axillae)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Multiple, deep-seated nodules and abscesses at different stages (inflamed, draining, scarred) in bilateral apocrine areas
  - Comedones (open and closed) – pathognomonic double-ended comedones (“tombstone” comedones)
  - Sinus tracts that are “ropelike” and bridged scarring
  - Positive “Tunnelling” sign: gentle compression produces purulent discharge from distal opening

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Furunculosis / simple boils** – no comedones, no sinus tracts, no bilateral symmetry; single lesion resolves with incision/drainage
- **Crohn’s disease with perianal fistulae** – look for GI symptoms (diarrhoea, weight loss); HS never involves perianal mucosa unless Crohn’s coexists
- **Perianal abscess / fistula-in-ano** – distinct anal gland origin; digital rectal exam reveals internal opening; HS usually spares the anal canal
- **Epidermoid cyst** – solitary, slow-growing, non-inflammatory unless ruptured; no recurrent crops
- **Lymphogranuloma venereum or Behçet’s disease** – sexual history and genital ulcers help differentiate
- **Splitter:** the presence of **double-ended comedones** in typical apocrine sites (axillae, groin) is virtually diagnostic of HS

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** 
  - Swab for culture (pus or draining sinus) to guide antibiotics if secondary infection suspected
  - Pain score (0–10) – HS is notoriously painful
- **Bloods:** 
  - FBC, CRP, ESR – often elevated during flares, but nonspecific
  - HbA1c, fasting glucose – HS is strongly associated with diabetes / insulin resistance
  - Lipid profile, BP – screen for metabolic syndrome
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** none routinely; ultrasound (high-frequency) can define sinus tract depth and exclude abscess collection if planning drainage
  - **Second-line:** MRI with contrast for complex disease (peri‑anal, perineal) to map sinus tracts before surgery
- **Special tests:** 
  - Consider biopsy if atypical (e.g., unilateral, no comedones, or persistent ulcer to exclude squamous cell carcinoma in chronic lesions)
- **Diagnostic criteria/scoring system:**
  - **Hurley staging (simplest for ward):**
    - **Stage I:** single or multiple abscesses without sinus tracts or scarring
    - **Stage II:** recurrent abscesses with sinus tracts and scarring, widely separated
    - **Stage III:** diffuse involvement with multiple interconnected sinus tracts and abscesses across entire region
  - **Sartorius severity score** (research) – counts nodules, sinuses, longest distance between lesions
- **Never-miss test:** **HbA1c** – undiagnosed diabetes is common in HS; miss it and you miss a modifiable risk factor and comorbidity

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If signs of sepsis (NEWS2 ≥5, fever, tachycardia): take blood cultures, start broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (e.g., co‑amoxiclav 1.2 g TDS or piperacillin‑tazobactam per local policy) after discussion with microbiology
  - Pain relief: paracetamol 1 g QDS + ibuprofen 400 mg TDS (if no contraindication) – many patients undermedicate
  - Incise and drain only if a fluctuant abscess with clear point of rupture and no deeper sinus tract; otherwise avoid – can worsen tracts
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **Mild (Hurley I):** topical clindamycin 1% lotion twice daily for 12 weeks; avoid long-term systemic antibiotics
  - **Moderate–severe (Hurley II–III):** 
    - Oral antibiotic combination: **clindamycin 300 mg twice daily + rifampicin 300 mg twice daily for 10 weeks** (NICE recommended)
    - Continue for minimum 10 weeks; reassess response at 4 weeks
    - If poor response: consider **adalimumab** (biologic; see Drugs section)
  - Wound care: gentle cleansing with saline, non-adherent dressings (e.g., Mepitel), avoid packing of sinus tracts – can extend tracts
  - Smoking cessation advice – proven to reduce flares
  - Weight management referral – obesity is a major driver
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Dermatology referral for all Hurley II–III
  - Biologics (adalimumab – first-line biologic in UK) or surgery (deroofing, local excision, or wide excision for advanced disease)
  - Consider oral tetracyclines (doxycycline 100 mg once daily for 3 months) as second-line if clindamycin/rifampicin not tolerated
  - Metformin (off-label) for insulin resistance (discuss with endocrinology)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** 
  - Fluids: if septic – 0.9% saline 500 mL stat, reassess; else maintenance fluids only if NBM
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) if BMI >30 (common in HS) – dose adjustment may be needed
  - Diet: normal, unless awaiting surgery
  - Observations: baseline and then per NEWS2, every 4 hours if stable

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line (moderate–severe):** 
  - **Clindamycin 300 mg orally twice daily + Rifampicin 300 mg orally twice daily for 10 weeks** (NICE CG157)
  - **Adalimumab** (when Hurley II–III and failed antibiotics): 
    - Loading dose: 160 mg SC at week 0, then 80 mg SC at week 2, then **40 mg weekly** from week 4
    - Pre-screen: TB, hepatitis B, HIV; monitor for infections
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Doxycycline 100 mg once daily** for 12 weeks (second-line, less effective)
  - **Erythromycin 500 mg twice daily** (if pregnancy or penicillin allergy)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Rifampicin: potent enzyme inducer; reduces efficacy of oral contraceptives, warfarin, many anticonvulsants – always check interactions
  - Clindamycin: risk of *Clostridium difficile* colitis – stop if diarrhoea develops
  - Adalimumab: avoid live vaccines; caution in active infections
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** 
  - Clindamycin: no dose adjustment needed in renal impairment; use caution in severe hepatic impairment (reduce dose by 50%)
  - Rifampicin: no adjustment in renal or hepatic
  - Adalimumab: no renal adjustment; caution in hepatic impairment (risk of hepatitis)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Rifampicin given as monotherapy** or short course (<10 weeks) – rapidly induces resistance; ALWAYS combine with clindamycin and complete full course
  - **Adalimumab loading dose given as 40 mg weekly from start** – leads to underdosing; ensure correct loading: 160 mg at week 0, 80 mg week 2, then 40 mg weekly

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Secondary infection: cellulitis, abscess, sepsis
  - Flare-related: severe pain, immobility, depression (high suicide risk)
  - Medication side effects: *C. difficile* colitis (clindamycin), hepatotoxicity (rifampicin), injection site reactions (adalimumab)
- **Chronic:**
  - Scarring: keloid, contractures (axillary or perineal, limiting movement)
  - Squamous cell carcinoma (rare, but high mortality) developing in chronic sinus tracts – any non-healing ulcer, especially in perineal disease, must be biopsied
  - Anaemia of chronic disease
  - Metabolic syndrome, cardiovascular events (increased risk)
  - Psychosocial: depression, social isolation, unemployment

### Prognosis
- Chronic relapsing disease with no cure; quality of life scores often as poor as in heart failure or severe psoriasis
- Early intervention (biologics, surgery) improves control; 30–50% of patients achieve remission with adalimumab
- Increased all-cause mortality (mainly cardiovascular and suicide)

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check HbA1c and fasting lipids** when you make the diagnosis – HS is a cutaneous marker of metabolic syndrome
- **Offer smoking cessation** at every interaction – a single most effective non-drug intervention
- **Refer to dermatology early** (Hurley II or III) – delays in biologics worsen scarring and outcome

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not incise and drain every lesion** – it can create new sinus tracts and worsen disease; only drain fluctuant, tense abscesses if point of rupture
- **Do not treat with short-course antibiotics** (e.g., 7 days of flucloxacillin) – HS requires 10 weeks of combination therapy (clindamycin + rifampicin) for moderate–severe
- **Do not dismiss as “just boils”** – HS has profound physical and psychological impact; take patient’s pain seriously and screen for depression

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** 
  - Spreading redness, fever, or rigors (signs of cellulitis/sepsis)
  - Unable to pass urine (associated with urethral stricture from perineal HS)
  - New severe pain or foul-smelling discharge (possible secondary infection)
- **Follow-up:**
  - Dermatology outpatient within 4 weeks for initial management plan (sooner if starting adalimumab)
  - General practice for regular monitoring of comorbidities (diabetes, lipids, BP)

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / medical team if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (especially if fever >38.5°C and CRP >100)
  - Signs of sepsis (lactate >2, hypotension) – start sepsis 6 within 1 hour
  - Suspicion of necrotising fasciitis (pain out of proportion, crepitus, bullae) – immediate surgical review
  - Suspected *C. difficile* colitis (diarrhoea after clindamycin) – stool culture and stop antibiotic
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or any single Red Score (respiratory rate >25, SPO2 <91%, systolic BP <90, new confusion)
  - Septic shock requiring vasopressors
